Outside of class, in addition to the Olympiad, I became Treasurer of my school’s National Honor Society and founded an investing club for 8th-grade students. Club members learned the basics of both economics and investing, and how good trades stem from clear sight of the market and its interplay with the overarching economy. I taught them like I taught myself, by utilizing diverse sources to simplify abstract jargon into simple graphs, patterns, and bullet points. Our team reached the top-20 regionally in the Stock Market Game, with one of my mentees landing in the top-10 individually. 

However, what inspires me the most are the hours-long discussions my father and I still have on everything economics, from breaking news to groundbreaking theories. For every theory I learned in class, he’s taught me lessons that go way beyond the textbook, drawing from his life experiences of starting a business with nothing but two siblings and a boatload of debt, failing, and trying again and again until they found success. Guided by his ingenuity and resilience, I will never forget that economics must be grounded in the experiences of everyday people.

topic: What would you say is your greatest talent or skill? How have you developed and demonstrated that talent over time?
content:
"I am disappointed." Right after the race, what was waiting for me was a shaking head and this line that was then imprinted on my mind.

I joined the swimming team in seventh grade to keep a childhood hobby alive. Fortunately, my coach especially looked out for me and was willing to work overtime to instruct me on swimming techniques. In just a year, I rapidly improved, and my coach felt I was ready for a regional competition. I was excited for my first competition; most of all, it was time to pay back my coach by doing well.

On the day of the competition, the pressure was palpable—I ruined my first-ever competition completely. Seeing my coach with his arms crossed over his chest sent shivers down my spine. That was also when he told me the words I would never forget.

However, my determination and commitment remained strong. The next day, I was the first to arrive at the pool. Training started at six in the morning. 2,000 meters to go, then a quick shower, and it was off to school. Back in the pool at five, the afternoon training schedule features an additional 3,000 meters. This became my routine for the year, no matter how sore my muscles were, how cold the weather was, or how infected my pink eye was.

Finally, the annual swimming competition came again. With the blow of a whistle, I leaped into the water. My mind was focused. With the blink of an eye, I touched the finishing wall. I looked toward the timer—I had just beaten my personal best record by almost five seconds. I leaped out of the water with tears in my eyes and gave my coach a big hug.

Swimming has taught me that the difference between success and failure is defined by the ability to turn obstacles into motivation and persist in what I do. With one-part optimism and one-part perseverance, my ability to reshape my adversities has become core to my identity and gives me the courage to face challenges and unknowns in the future.

topic: Think about an academic subject that inspires you. Describe how you have furthered this interest inside and/or outside of the classroom.
content:
Surrounded by a sea of obsidian static, the alabaster outline of a fetus materialized, his soft silhouette and organic curves painted by perfectly squared pixels on display. At a young age, this was my introduction to medical imaging. Eyes glued to screen, I was mesmerized by how a culmination of plastics and circuit boards revealed the genesis of new life, all through a probe gliding smoothly along an expectant woman’s stomach. With my father often absent and my mother always busy, most of my after-school hours were spent at the gynecology clinic she worked at. The four walls of her office quickly became the cradle for my interest in medical imaging as I quietly observed her work, absorbing the process of monitoring the earliest stages of our lives.

My fascination with medical imaging continued as a guiding light throughout high school, serving as the fuel behind countless cold emails until I was accepted as a research assistant at a preclinical brain imaging lab. Under whirling MRI machines that unravel the secrets of brain autonomy, I learned to apply medical imaging to another context—investigating the neuroplasticity of adolescent rats. Over the summer, my eyes were opened to the potential of neuroscience in transforming patient outcomes. 

I continued to pursue my developing interest in neuroscience through a graduate-level course, where I learned about the prominence of congenital disorders in developing countries. As I prepared for a presentation on neural tube defects, I discovered the devastating effects of holoprosencephaly, a fetal developmental disorder often linked to maternal folate deficiency in developing countries. As I sifted through articles, the haunting images of affected children brought me to a heartbreaking realization that much of their suffering could have been prevented with adequate resources and healthcare. 

This was the day I knew that I wanted to be a neurosurgeon. 

Neuroscience is not just about understanding the brain, but bringing hope to those in need. My ultimate goal is to serve as a doctor without borders, and I know my next step as a neuroscience major will equip me with the tools to transform vision into reality.

topic: What have you done to make your school or your community a better place?
content:
Stepping into the local hospital sophomore year summer, I embraced the role of student volunteer, eager to assist patients. The sterile scent of alcohol greeted me and the vast, maze-like hospital left me disoriented, so I struggled to find the desired locations in the labyrinthine hallways. After hours, I pored over the map to memorize the hospital layout as well as nearby clinics. After a few days, a cognitive map materialized in my mind. 

With newfound assurance, I took the initiative to approach lost patients without guidance. Volunteers are always paired with senior volunteers, but I was so familiar with the departments and at ease with patients that the senior volunteers trusted me to work alone. I was also given the task of inspecting the 1000+ wheelchairs, transporting broken ones to the basement repair center. Noticing that a mere ten wheelchairs were available at the main entrance at any given time, I challenged myself to bring wheelchairs from the parking lot to the front gate after volunteer hours concluded and before my bus showed up. I taught myself to push two wheelchairs at a time, ensuring that at least ten readily available wheelchairs greeted patients.

Returning the following summer, I felt a sense of homecoming. The senior volunteers could not believe it was only my “first” day back as I efficiently led patients to their desired locations. I particularly focused on taking care of senior patients with disabilities, personally managing their care when they came without their children. Leading them, I felt as though I was positively contributing to the hospital even in a sea of seasoned healthcare professionals. 

I now aspire to serve the community by becoming a doctor and embedding myself within the fabric of the hospital. The intimate understanding of patients’ struggles, the satisfaction of offering support, and the profound impact healthcare professionals have all deeply resonate with me. I also hope to understand and crack my own Tourette’s condition for myself and others. Going into medicine, I dedicate myself to healing, guiding, and making a tangible difference in the lives of those in need.

topic: Think about an academic subject that inspires you. Describe how you have furthered this interest inside and/or outside of the classroom.
content:
"What is the equation for a circle?"

My teacher posted a question before the class was dismissed. Being an eighth grader then, this became the one thing that troubled me for the day, as my textbook itself simply could not provide the answer to this question: I went on a math spree where all I thought about was equations and circles. Eventually, my light-bulb moment appeared when the Pythagorean theorem popped into my mind, and a sense of accomplishment came to me when I successfully derived the equation of a circle. In fact, this rather simple question in my algebra class set off an enlightening journey into the world of math.

I have always had a natural curiosity when it comes to numbers. To me, it is like solving a jigsaw puzzle where I pick up my knowledge piece by piece and place them together to see the whole picture of problems and come up with solutions. Moreover, while the derivation of a circle equation demonstrated to me the unimaginable possibilities of utilizing math, I began to develop a profound interest in the subject and became motivated to take all the challenging courses that involved math offered at my school.

To my surprise, I fell further into math when I expanded my puzzle-solving into computer science and physics. During these courses, it was fascinating to see the interconnectedness between subjects, where different equations come together and explain to me the numerous natural phenomena. I vividly recall when calculating the unknown values of current and voltages in the circuit, where I picked up my knowledge of matrices from linear algebra and placed it together with Kirchhoff's laws, and the answer to the question became more than obvious, whereas in programming, mathematical logic is the key to solving complex problems and making efficient programs. 

Through various math-related courses, while enhancing my knowledge of solving numerical equations, applying different mathematical methods taught me the numerous possibilities of solving a single question, allowing me to better understand the world around me and giving me the ability to attempt to solve problems and provide solutions.

topic: Describe the most significant challenge you’ve faced and the steps you’ve taken to overcome this challenge. How has this challenge affected your academic achievement?
content:
At the age of 7, tics (spontaneous muscle twitches and random vocal sounds) started to characterize my behavior, causing irritation and even mockery from others. Living abroad in Shanghai, where tics were heavily stigmatized, kids would imitate me. 

This condition? Tourette’s Syndrome.

Discouraged, I found solace by imagining myself in other people's shoes. Seeing someone else shouting arbitrarily would annoy me too. However, as I grew older, the symptoms got worse, and so did the reactions. My head would shake uncontrollably while studying, making it difficult to concentrate on tasks, especially reading. Diverting my attention, these random headshakes impacted my academic performance. At school, I was put in detention, and at the mall, people called me "psycho." In eighth grade, I did research on the disorder and discovered that exercise and vitamins might suppress the symptoms. Behavioral therapy suggested consciously preventing oneself from releasing tics, which made me even more stressed. All these methods failed to provide lasting relief, leaving me overwhelmed and frustrated.

For a fresh start, I lived with a host family in LA for freshman year and did online school. However, my tics resurfaced with increased force. Fearing that I would be ostracized in the US after going back to in-person school, I moved back to Taiwan to be with my family. There, I stumbled upon a particular solution: badminton. When I played this sport, my tics significantly decreased. It was a revelation. Immersed in something I loved, my mind ceased to fight against Tourette’s relentless assault, and my tics went away. I could spend entire afternoons playing badminton, relishing the joy of sweat, free of shakes.

Inspired by this newfound freedom, I focused less on fretting and more on my academic pursuits. Free of fear, I immersed myself in school and developed a genuine love for learning, which became the most meaningful achievement of all. I even started tutoring my peers in biology and math. Now, I want to become a doctor to both embrace Tourette’s as a natural phenomenon and to find methods to mitigate the impact of such disorders on myself and others.

topic: Think about an academic subject that inspires you. Describe how you’ve furthered this interest inside and/or outside the classroom.
content:
From an early age, I was captivated by the ability to wield power over life and death. Cartoons and documentaries depicting remarkable physicians engaged in intricate medical feats filled my imagination. Enchanted by these narratives, I’d lose myself for hours while reading, much like any child absorbed in the magic of fictional worlds.

Living with the intermittent tics of Tourette’s Syndrome often puzzled me. Freshman year biology unveiled that all cellular DNA is identical, yet manifest diverse traits through gene expression. Continuing my exploration of biology throughout high school, I competed in the British Biology Olympiad in 2022 and 2023 and won Silver both years. Biology and math go hand in hand, so I also competed in the Singapore & Asian Schools Math Olympiad 2023, winning Bronze. During junior year summer, I enrolled in a biology summer course with Professor Arri Eisen and discovered the realm of epigenetics, in which genes respond to environmental cues. The epiphany struck: perhaps epigenetics was key in triggering my Tourette’s in response to stressors!

That summer, I did research with Dr. Eisen, exploring the microbiome’s interaction with SARS-CoV-2. I theorized in a paper that microbes can suppress symptom manifestation and proposed an experiment to validate this concept. In college, I envision myself fully engaging in the world of biological research at a lab, collaborating with like-minded peers and accomplished mentors to explore epigenetics further. The realm of epigenetics has led me to believe that understanding how genes react to environmental cues can potentially unravel complex disorders of all kinds.

In my life, biology has become more than a subject; it’s an inspiration that fuels my curiosity and thinking as I examine and apply the mechanisms to different scenarios. At school, I act as a Biology TA, helping to design class experiments. I tutor my classmates in biology and have been told my enthusiasm encourages my students to learn as well. Guided by my passion, I aspire to unravel more of life’s intricacies as a doctor, applying my knowledge to improve everyone's health. Biology, once merely a collection of captivating tales, now embodies my academic zeal.

topic: What have you done to make your school or your community a better place?
content:
As the child of an elementary school teacher, I have always wanted to have a chance to teach others to feel the responsibility students will never have.

My dream finally came true in eighth grade when I had the opportunity to teach English to elementary children from local Taiwanese schools in a book reading club. In the beginning, the students barely understood the stories and the language, but slowly, the confused look on their faces faded as we read more books and their vocabulary expanded. This experience made me realize the fun of teaching does not come from the privilege of having my students listen to or follow my words; instead, it comes from the fulfilling smile on their faces when they are able to follow along with the stories.

Back in school, I volunteered as a student assistant in the fourth-grade class and continued to help others with their English. Yet, it was not that easy this time. A Japanese girl in the class was having a hard time keeping up with the lessons and refused to communicate with me at first when I tried to help out. I then devised a plan for striking up a conversation with her by bringing up her favorite animal. With my successful attempt, I began to teach her English after school and tried my best to translate course materials into Japanese with the help of technology. Admittedly, my Japanese translations were often far from perfect; however, I saw the improvement in her English, where she became able to understand in class and hold conversations with others.

Outside of school, I joined a peer tutoring program for lower-income students and was paired with a local high school student who had a clear goal to pass the intermediate-level Taiwanese General English Proficiency Test. We broke down each section of the test to identify concepts that she was unfamiliar with, and she eventually passed the test. Meanwhile, I came to realize the importance of understanding the condition that people are in through communication to better help them adapt to various situations and achieve their goals.

topic: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
content:
The bustling hallways of the iGEM Grand Jamboree in Paris were alive with excitement and innovation when I first met him—a brilliant intellect who seemed to know everything about biology. In November before my senior year, this event would ignite my passion for synthetic biology. Amidst the whirlwind of activity, Laurent, the Primary Instructor of GEMS-Taiwan, approached our small booth. I stood alone, representing our team of eight from WIST_iGEM, and explained our project on combating soft rot disease. Laurent’s insightful questions and profound understanding of biology impressed not only me but his entire team.

After the Grand Jamboree, I was both flattered and shocked when Laurent contacted me in December, inviting me to join GEMS-Taiwan for the 2024 iGEM competition. Despite his casual mention of this possibility in Paris, I had taken it as a joke. Receiving the invitation was a surreal moment, especially knowing the high regard in which GEMS-Taiwan was held. Unlike my previous team, GEMS-Taiwan was large, with 31 members compared to WIST’s eight, and everyone else was from schools in Taipei, whereas I was the only one from a different city.

Shortly after I joined, Laurent had to step down due to family issues. This was a significant blow since he was incredibly knowledgeable and had been a mentor figure. Surprisingly, none of my new teammates knew who Laurent was, as the team had new members each year. We still had other instructors—Jakie for Wetlab, Timmy for Drylab, KJ for software, and Jessie for Human Practices—but Laurent’s absence was felt keenly by me.

Despite initial challenges, the team environment was incredibly welcoming. During the topic selection phase, where each member proposed ideas, I was delighted when someone chose to join my topic, which eventually became the second topic in our selection process. This gesture of support made me feel included and motivated. Personally, I became more open and communicative, no longer afraid to share my thoughts. Professionally, I drew on my experiences from WIST_iGEM to offer practical suggestions, particularly for our human practices team.

One of the most memorable moments with GEMS-Taiwan was our educational outreach program. Visiting several high schools to promote synthetic biology, I felt anxious due to my introverted nature. However, my teammates, especially Sophie, who was incredibly extroverted and friendly, encouraged me to stay calm and assured me everything would be fine. This support transformed our outreach efforts into a significant success, allowing us to develop comprehensive education plans, interview strategies, and contact lists.

My journey with iGEM has profoundly shaped my future aspirations. Engaging in synthetic biology research and projects has opened my eyes to the vast possibilities within microbiology. Thanks to the experiences and relationships I built with GEMS-Taiwan, I am now determined to pursue a career in microbiology. Special thanks to Sophie, whose friendliness helped me bridge the gap with my new teammates and find my voice within the team.

Though Laurent is no longer part of the team, his initial belief in me set me on this path. The experiences and connections I’ve gained have been invaluable, both personally and professionally. My journey from WIST_iGEM to GEMS-Taiwan has taught me resilience, the importance of a supportive environment, and the power of mentorship. These lessons will guide me as I continue my journey in synthetic biology.

topic: The lessons we take from obstacles we encounter can be fundamental to later success. Recount a time when you faced a challenge, setback, or failure. How did it affect you, and what did you learn from the experience?
content:
On a dark night when everyone was asleep, my room was still full of light. On the table, nothing was organized. An almost drained laptop, a thick biology textbook, teacher’s handouts, and notebook papers were spread all over the table. A tired girl sat mumbling missense, frameshift, and 3-nucleotide-pair deletion in a low and exhausted voice. Suddenly, the girl lifted up her head with bright eyes, drawing explanation images in her notebook, adding colors with highlighters in a frenzy. This was me, in two extreme states: distressed to excited. Finally, I understood how the four bases A, T, C, G lined up in DNA in different combinations, and what made viable versus jangled genes. 

The dramatic change from distressed and exhausted to exhilarated hadn’t come from nowhere. The key for staying up so long and late was to stay calm and patient - more than ten years of piano had taught me that. I love to play fast and feverish music like Caprice Espagnol Op.37 by Moszkowski. In appearance, pianists need to be wild and powerful in order to master these pieces. However,  I need to be tranquil and detailed when I am learning every note so that I would not mess up the complex chores and melodies. 

My journey to loving science was a winding one. As a child, I was inspired by doctors. To me, they were angels that combined the broken pieces of families destroyed by disease. I wanted to become others’ sunshine and bring happiness to people around me. But my passion was nearly killed after failing STEM test after STEM test despite my best efforts in local school. I started to consider whether I should give up my childhood dream and face reality. 

There was far more to worry about than losing my childhood dream when I transferred from Chinese local school to international school. Years of learning history via memorizing years and names made me a stranger to learning this subject using critical thinking and analytical writing. When I took AP Biology, I didn't even recognize ‘photosynthesis’, because I had only studied it in Chinese before.  Initially I tried to pour knowledge into my head at the fastest pace possible by copying as many technical terms as I could in my notebook, regardless of whether I understood their meaning. Like Detective Rizzoli in Tess Gerritsen’s I Know A Secret, I was neglecting crucial evidence by focusing all my efforts on one method: she gathers huge amounts of evidence for Ketamine as a key in the murders, ignoring crucial evidence that she could have noticed if she had considered other methods. 

I remembered the lesson from Caprice Espagnol. I started to focus on how much information I had grasped instead of how many pages of the textbook I had read; I spent lab and after school time with my teacher to discuss cell structures and cycles. Over time, I did not study science purely via memorizing, but tried to connect everything in a never-ending story about life and Earth. 

These days, I intern at Chang-Gung Memorial hospital’s immunology laboratory. I see the researchers facing the same frustrations I have confronted: out of 10 experiments, 7-8 will fail. The researchers have dark circles and sloppy hair, pipetting everyday without breakthroughs. But they are not irritated by the results: instead, they calmly ponder about why experiments fail. 

From them, I see that calm is the key to overcoming obstacles. It’s a lesson that has served me in the World Scholar’s Cup, where three patient years of weekly debates and practice changed me from a fearful speaker to a confident debater who can even hold the position of president. It’s a lesson I know I’ll carry with me: life will continue to give me countless challenges, but if I face them with a humble and calm heart, behind these sharp thorns, there are priceless gifts waiting for me.

topic: Describe a topic, idea, or concept you find so engaging that it makes you lose all track of time. Why does it captivate you? What or who do you turn to when you want to learn more?
content:
The epitome of femininity is perhaps when a woman tenderly and delicately handles everything she touches with love and care. But, to my parents’ dismay, nothing left my hands unscathed when I was young thanks to my habit of dismantling. Hence, I earned the title of “Property Destroyer” from my mom, which, at the time, was anything but affectionate.

Each time I stealthily snuck off with my dad’s screwdriver set to take something apart, I envisioned myself a brilliant inventor who could reassemble seemingly trivial parts into breathtaking gadgets. A source of endless headaches for my parents, my habit of dismantling objects was really a manifestation of this ambitious dream to become an inventor. But with each failed trial and each scolding I received–deservedly so, especially the time I took apart a brand new, lustrously white toaster–I learned to give up on this dream.

However, my unconscious mind continued to drift towards my naive dream. As I sat listening to a molecular biology lecture, I inevitably thought about how one might take advantage of hydrogen atoms’ adhesive properties. Physics class had currents of air swirling around my brain as I made sense of how to capitalize on their movements. At home, I saw my family’s sole worn-out air purifier; “Property Destroyer” pounded on the door of her oppressive cell, begging to take a crack at improving its functionality. But I quieted that voice, patiently doing more research before breaking anything.

 The filters I found were neither cost-efficient nor environmentally friendly. It then hit me: instead of expensive, disposable filters, why not use water as a natural filter? Its adhesive properties would make for the perfect medium to collect dust particles. I quickly researched to see if this had been done before; it had, but the results were unsatisfying. None of the few designs I saw were elegant or could be manufactured affordably.

 

Thus, my endeavor of inventing a water-based purifier began. I ripped apart a small, broken humidifier I had and took out the mist atomizer and circuit board it was attached to.

 

Next, I would need something to pull out the air to speed up the process. I glanced at my closet door. Inside was a box of dismantled items, intended to be kept hidden from my sight—every sight of it only served as a brutal reminder of my unrealistic, childish dream. But today, it had something I needed. I opened the box, taking out a mini-fan that would be perfect for the job. For the physical structure of the air purifier, I decided to connect a short plastic box – which collects water – to a long, clear one that would make the mist visible.

 

With each prototype came new components and mistakes: new layers in the body of the purifier to make filtration more effective, a LED bulb that illuminated the mist into different colors and turned it into a nightlight. The water would leak or the fan would stop from improperly bonded wires. But this was exciting to me: I was just like the inventors and engineers who had come before me, creating perfect designs only after making a million tweaks.

Eventually, I reached a prototype I was content with and applied for a patent. My patent was granted, and I earned the judges’ approval at international invention fairs! While I pretend to be annoyed whenever my friends jokingly call me Ms. Thomas Edison, I cannot help but ignore the rush of fulfillment each time I hear it.

I wear my title of “Property Destroyer” proudly now. Innovation will always be a clumsy process filled with failed attempts and discarded pieces for me, but I know I can fearlessly confront the challenges ahead. And along the way, my feats of engineering may even improve the quality of life for others.

topic: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
content:
Four years ago, I couldn’t have imagined myself wearing an ugly khaki uniform, sitting on a log starting a fire from scratch. The thought of that uniform gave me goosebumps. Little did I know that it was going to spark a period of personal growth and an inner realization for me.

Back then, I was like most boys: we liked to act cool, be on the cutting edge. If everyone was going to the movies with their friends, I was doing that too. I even started playing the game PUBG because everyone else was, despite not enjoying it at all. My grades suffered as a result, but I was convinced that if I didn’t, I wouldn’t be cool, wouldn’t have friends, and would be worthless. 

My dad – not a middle schooler – obviously did not care about such things. Having grown up on a farm, he thought I wasted too much time with gadgets in our tech-filled city. So, when my friend from elementary school visited us to recruit for the Boy Scouts, my dad and I had opposite reactions: his face lit up and eyes opened wide, while a bad feeling spread throughout my entire body. It was like he had been waiting for this moment, whereas all I could think of was: scouting? What a lame thing.

I didn’t even get the chance to resist, though. My dad dragged me to the very next Saturday Boy Scouts meeting. Like a student attending a lecture on a complex topic they have no prior knowledge of, I sat through the whole thing questioning why I was there. Even worse, it was all in English, and I was too embarrassed to speak out loud. I hated everything.

A few weeks later, my mood was only souring as raindrops pattered on my dad’s car and the radio wailed sad classical music. I could only think about how lame I looked in my uniform as we drove further and further into the middle of nowhere. When we finally arrived at Nowhere, it was already 6 and the sun had set. Getting out of the car, I immediately sneezed as the cold wrapped its arms around me. Then I heard my name.

 The call had come from a group of boys sitting around a campfire, all waving at me. Looking at them, I thought the uniforms actually looked good in the forest, and suddenly, I felt a little less embarrassed about wearing mine. As I took my place among them and joined the conversation, they seemed like me: teenage boys who wanted to be cool and liked. But there was also something that was different between them. It was the thing that had made me so resistant and worried instead of taking the chance to laugh and make new friends. It was the thing that made them seem so cool and at ease in those dark woods.

 It was confidence. I realized then— it was all confidence. The reason why I thought Boy Scouts was so humiliating was because I lacked confidence in myself. For a confident man, what mattered most was how you viewed yourself.

Four years later, I’ve become one of the most experienced scouts in the troop. Since committing to the scouts, I have tried to live the life that I want and do things that I like rather than just what is popular. It hasn’t always been easy: I lost a lot of attention and friends after I tried to live more confidently and truthfully. But I know the friends who stuck with me through it all are true. There are still times when I find myself falling back into old habits, choosing things because they seem popular, and I want to fit in.

Mostly, though, I’m proud to don the uniform and teach the younger kids how they, too, can have the courage just to be themselves.

topic: The lessons we take from obstacles we encounter can be fundamental to later success. Recount a time when you faced a challenge, setback, or failure. How did it affect you, and what did you learn from the experience?
content:
“I am disappointed.” One of the people I cared most about shook his head as I approached him after the race. His words imprinted into my mind. 

 

I joined the swimming team in 7th grade to keep a childhood hobby alive. My teammates, however, had all started in elementary school and were all national and regional champions—much due to Coach Chang, who always held everyone to high standards. It was immediately clear how behind I was. However, Coach especially looked out for me, working overtime to instruct me on swimming techniques. In just a year, I rapidly improved, and Coach Chang felt I was ready for a regional competition. I was excited for my first competition; most of all, it was time to pay back my coach by doing well.

 

The competition pool was much larger than the one I usually trained at. Seeing the other athletes made my legs quiver: they were all taller, stronger, fitter than me. The pressure was palpable. With the blow of a whistle, I was stunned on land; with those first few seconds of delay, I’d completely ruined my first-ever competition. Getting out of the pool and seeing Coach standing there with his arms crossed over his chest and legs firmly planted on the ground sent shivers down my spine; his shoulders were tense, and his gaze was piercing. That is when Coach told me the words I would never forget. 

 

I did not return to training the next day. My phone was filled with unread messages from my swim mates asking where I’d been; I sealed myself in my room, staring into the abyss as pieces of the competition played over in my head. My love for swimming retreated as those three words echoed in my brain. Suddenly, my phone buzzed: it was Coach. I ended the call. A few seconds later, Coach called again. I reluctantly picked up, and there were no harsh words, no criticisms. Instead, he told me his story.

He had trained since he was a toddler, always putting swimming before everything else. But one day, he had a blowout argument with his father. Only later did he realize what an advantage the extreme training, harsh words, and isolation his father put him through were. However, even if Coach wanted to swim again, his body had already passed its prime. Coach said, “Giving up is never an option if you want to achieve something great; by the time you start regretting, it might be too late.” His words were an epiphany: I no longer wanted to be the quitter I was.

 

The next day, I woke up at five and went straight to training. I spent approximately five hours each day in the pool. For a year, no matter how sore my muscles were, how cold the weather was, or how infected my pink eye was, my determination to train remained resolute. 

Finally, the annual swimming competition came again. This time, I had experience, confidence, and skills built over two years. With the blow of a whistle, I leaped into the bone-shriveling water. My mind was focused: there was only the water and me. With the blink of an eye, I touched the finishing wall. I looked around me, and seeing no one, I knew I had finished first in my group. When I looked at the timer and saw I had finally beaten my personal best record by almost five seconds, I leaped out of the water with tears in my eyes and gave Coach a big hug. Words can't describe how grateful I was to be able to return the dedication and patience Coach had shown me.

 

That was the second moment imprinted in my mind forever: Coach Chang’s look of pride as I climbed out of the pool. It was the greatest proof of the power of perseverance, something I won’t ever let go of.

topic: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story
content:
There’s a force that pulls my mind away. Starting at seven years old, my muscles would twitch spontaneously, my head would tremble intermittently, my thoughts would be consumed by unrelenting fixations, and I would be filled with urges to twitch and screech. I have always stood apart from my peers due to my tics and its peculiar cycle of dormancy and manifestation. Living abroad in Shanghai, where physical conditions are still heavily stigmatized, my classmates ostracized me, while my teachers punished me with detention. The most disconcerting incidents occurred in public spaces. I vividly remember a time when I screeched as I entered a mall, and bystanders branded me a “psycho.” Such episodes were commonplace in my life. 

My condition? Tourette’s Syndrome. 

Upon introspection, imagining myself in someone else’s shoes, I realized that I would feel irritation as well. I felt solace in this understanding, but the stigma was still hard to bear. As a rising freshman, I decided to attend high school in the US to practice my English and have a fresh start. I lived with a host family while the rest of my family moved back to Taiwan, my home country. Online school during COVID was already difficult, and I found MUN especially challenging. To my dismay, stress exacerbated my tics. After years of latency, they returned with heightened intensity. I emitted inexplicable noises daily, causing my host parents and roommates to voice discontent. The silence of the night amplified my premonitory urges; trying to suppress my tics, I grappled even more with the desire to make sounds. The more I dwelled on these urges, the harder they became to suppress. Paradoxically, the more I disregarded them, the more consistently the tics surfaced. These conflicting symptoms burdened me, and I struggled to focus on school. Overwhelmed with uncertainty, I wondered if the host family would kick me out. I was a newcomer — why should they tolerate me?

The time came to confront the situation. My host parents, expressing concern, offered to take me to a doctor. Despite knowing that Tourette’s had no cure, I consented to taking vitamins and fish oil supplements in the spirit of cooperation. Nothing worked. Eventually, the news of my spasms reached my parents, although I had not wanted them, an ocean away, to worry about me. My condition continued to linger, and I confined myself to my room for days on end in embarrassment and shame. 

As the school year drew to a close, I returned to Taiwan to be with my family. I discovered a captivating sport — badminton. To my astonishment, the severity of my tics decreased when I played; this was a miracle. Upon reflection, I realized that the solution did not lie within textbooks or research, but simply in a change of mindset. Immersed in something I loved, my mind ceased to struggle with Tourette’s relentless assault, and the tics went away. I could spend entire afternoons playing badminton, relishing the joy of sweat and lack of shakes. 

By ceasing to fret about my tics and accepting them as an integral part of me, they became a catalyst for personal growth. In the words of Hellen Keller, “Once I fretted and beat myself against the wall that shut me in. Now I rejoice in the consciousness that I can think, act and attain heaven.” Like her, I’ve come to rejoice in the strength and resilience I have developed to navigate life’s challenges. Determined to overcome this disorder, I have pursued my aspirations with unwavering determination, embracing the obstacles that lie ahead. 

This journey made me realize the pivotal role healthcare providers play in medical challenges. Over the years, my doctors have been pillars of hope. Between my own fight with Tourette's and my empathy towards those with physical and mental struggles, my experiences have kindled a passion to pursue a medical career to help others in need.

topic: Some students have a background, identity, interest, or talent that is so meaningful they believe their application would be incomplete without it. If this sounds like you, then please share your story.
content:
Today morning, at three 2pm. I took a long breath, slowing walking to the door - I gotta meet my therapist. 

I believe that there is a soul waiting to be explored in everything. This is what I seek in life, whether it’s in the realm of robotics, music, or society. I believe that the presence of a soul infuses meaning and a purpose in life. I came to this realization in the summer camp at Boston University Tanglewood Institute.

“Music is not just melody; it’s not just chords. Your performance lacks soul,” my violin teacher told me every lesson. The soul, it’s the missing piece I’m looking for in my music. 

In the past, I used to believe that techniques created connotations in music, positioning the indispensable rule in the content of the piece. However, I often felt like a blind man touching a ball, aware of its material and size but unable to perceive its color. I tried to furrow my brow to immerse myself in the music and emphasize vibrato in my performances. In fact, these approaches backfired my performance.  I felt the voice, “Your performance lacks soul” engraved into my mind again and again. At that moment, I felt I was faking emotions and a flood of failure overwhelmed me. 

Amidst my confusion and sense of being lost, the appearance of a summer camp became a turning point. during the BUTI summer camp in Massachusetts, that's when I discovered the color of the ball.

The composition “Ashe”, which means "so let it be." was crafted by a modern composer Valerie Coleman. Although it seemed ordinary with a slow tempo and ordinary rhythm, when I first played it, I felt overwhelmed. It stirred a desire within me to ask the composer, "What have you been through?" Beginning with the major key, the melody carried a hint of sadness and helplessness. It felt as if the composer herself was lost, slowly traversing a solitary path, grappling with suffering she couldn't express—an ambivalence weighing on her. Although I couldn't fully comprehend the composer's true inner thoughts, I felt a deep connection. It was as if I engaged in a conversation with her, attempting to understand her journey. The composition included harmonies that evoked unresolved tension, but the following section's melody filled the previous gaps, creating a sense of relief. These subtle changes convey the composer’s suffering and recovery. This soul in the piece transcends time, space, and realms, enabling me to resonate with the mood behind it, transcending all limitations.

On the day of the performance at Ozawa Hall, tears welled up in my eyes. They were not tears of sadness or sorrow, but tears of experiencing the very essence of music—the ability to communicate with my own soul and exchange inner emotions with others. It transcended the confines of time, space, and place, allowing me to delve into the depths of my emotions alongside the composer and listen to the feelings we shared. At that moment of realization, I no longer relied on additional music techniques to comprehend the soul of the music. I spontaneously witnessed the true color of the ball, and I could vividly describe it.

Life is not just fulfilled with technology and progression. Even in science and technology, every successful invention underlies a distinct purpose that touches the human soul. Robotics, a field that fascinates me, is not only composed of speed, extensive data, or the acquisition of professional knowledge. Instead, its true essence lies in its capacity to serve as a bridge, creating a soul/spirit that makes humans willing to interact with it. If a robot lacks emotion and temperature, a human would prefer to interact with a real dog, which can only memorize no more than 200 words.

topic: The lessons we take from obstacles we encounter can be fundamental to later success. Recount a time when you faced a challenge, setback, or failure. How did it affect you, and what did you learn from the experience?
content:
I was born and raised in a conventional Taiwanese family, with my mother being an elementary teacher and my father being an engineer working in the science park. I have an older brother, and we are the closest buddies outside school. Yet, my family has this unique tradition of participating in voluntary work every month, such as mountain cleanup, since my parents are volunteers for the Taiwanese Forest Bureau. However, it wasn’t always the case back in eighth grade, when our family encountered a particularly challenging period.

It was the moment my world shifted when my mother was diagnosed with stage three cancer. As a middle schooler, I was initially unaware of the gravity of the situation. Nevertheless, it wasn’t until I saw her go through chemotherapy that the full seriousness of her condition became painfully clear. Her once vibrant countenance grew pale, and she lost all appetite, ushering in a difficult journey for our family. It was devastating and overwhelming for me to see my mom, the strong pillar of my family on whom my brother and I could always fall back, losing all her hair and becoming quite frail. Furthermore, due to my mother’s condition, my father had to accompany her to the hospital constantly; my brother and I had to be self-reliant and sensible and began to shoulder full responsibility for housework while maintaining our schooling, wanting to support our mother in any way we could, even if it was the slightest. In fact, I wasn’t always the obedient child like my brother growing up; I was the troublemaker whom my parents worried about all the time. I often argued with them over insignificant things, thinking they were grumpy adults who could never take my opinions into account. Yet, my mother’s diagnosis made me reevaluate my attitude and behaviors toward them, especially toward my mom, as it made me comprehend what grownups mean when they say, “Life is short; cherish everyone around you and don’t take their existence for granted.” Despite it being challenging and devastating for me and my family, I did not lose hope for my mother's situation and the treatments she was going through, believing she was a strong woman who would eventually overcome this drawback one day.

After a long year of treatment, up until now, my mother still has to return to the hospital every two to three months for follow-up examinations. Every time she comes back, I will again ask her how the examination went, and like most of the times where she responds that everything is fine, it seems like a stone has dropped from my heart. Indeed, my mother's time fighting over cancer has left an indelible mark on my family as well as on me myself. In the past, it was usually my mother who took care of me no matter what, but her health condition had a significant impact on my personal growth and prompted a significant transformation in my role. I have transitioned into a young “adult” who now has the ability to independently manage my responsibilities and affairs, taking on the role of not only looking after myself but also tending to our family’s needs and obligations.

In the end, while our family was finally able to return to our dedication to regular volunteering activities, I began to appreciate more the small things around me that I used to take for granted, which made me discover the joy of helping people and planted a seed of passion for nature, motivating me to partake in numerous voluntary activities and take on initiatives to make positive impacts in my community and beyond.

topic: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
content:
If you asked me where you should visit if you’re in Taiwan, I would have to recommend one of the crown gems of Formosa: the mangrove forests at Taijiang National Park. Kayaking through the green tunnels of the mangrove forests was a therapeutic experience. It was almost as if I was conversing with nature. However, one year, outside noise interrupted our dialogue. Dozens of dead fish littered the surface of the water, turning the once scenic tunnels into sepulchers. Later on, the news revealed the culprit: fertilizer leakage.

	

This marred childhood memory was one of my first brush with the often-fraught relationship between humanity and nature. Over time, my interest in nature flowered into a deep passion. However, I was not always an avid environmentalist. I once thought that nature was an object to be subjugated and bent to the will of Man. 

I did not start my passion for the outdoors with an environmental mindset. I used to be solely focused on my personal achievements and collecting successful hikes as if they were Pokemon. After I fell off the side of a cliff due to an ice screw not being properly anchored, I became even more motivated to assert my dominance over nature. I solo climbed a 10,000-foot mountain every two weeks and was one of the youngest Taiwanese hikers to ever summit all ten of the highest peaks on the island. Logging my conquered peaks on my Garmin watch gave me a feeling of accomplishment. 

However, my worldview changed while climbing Ergeshan, a particularly challenging climb that required expert technical skills. While trying to secure myself into an arete, I fell off yet another cliff. It was at this moment that I had two realizations. First, the frequency of me falling off cliffs was much too high. Second, I needed to seriously reconsider my relationship with nature. As I laid in a hospital bed over the next week in a pain-killer induced trance, I wondered if I would ever be able to climb mountains again.

After a 6 month long rehabilitation process, I slowly regained my ability to hike and began to tackle easier mountains again. While I took on these easy hikes, I experienced a bizarre Taiwanese phenomenon: elderly hikers passing you on trails and shouting encouragement to you. They would breeze past me in their flip flops and give me a pat on the back while saying “Are you hiking solo? Nice job, young man!”Overtime, I joined this community of freakishly fit seniors and learned many lessons along the way. 

They told me stories about the history of Taiwan that gave me a deeper understanding of the trails that had been paved by the generations before me. I learned that these paths did not simply connect point A to point B but reflected the rich history of our country. They echoed a time before the arrival of the Kuomintang and bore witness to the native beauty of the island. As the seniors and I climbed together, my appreciation for history and nature took on new heights.

I also discovered that climate change was no far-away threat but a real and present danger to our environment. I saw lush forests and meadows fall dry and barren. From the tales of my senior hiking companions, I learned that these changes were merely the tip of the melting iceberg. This realization left me with the pressing need to take action to prevent further devastation.

My community of senior hikers taught me valuable lessons and granted me a new attitude towards people, history, and nature. 

Someday, in a far-off future, I hope to be one of the kind seniors passing by a young hiker and saying:

“Are you hiking solo? Nice job, young man!”.

topic: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
content:
Aromatic vapor flooded my senses as I lifted the hefty iron lid of the rice cooker. I was greeted by the pleasant sight of steamed egg, its perfectly smooth surface glistening in the dull kitchen light like polished yellow jade. Grandpa had always been in charge of preparing meals at home- from porridge to rice cakes, his cooking always managed to light up our faces with joyful expressions of contentment. His steamed eggs were my favorite– despite its simple recipe, the tender and delicate texture was a spoonful of comfort that balanced the savory relish and rich, buttery taste of eggs. However, as Grandpa aged, the flavor of the steamed eggs became inconsistent by the day. On certain days, the eggs tasted as if someone had dumped in a gallon of the Dead Sea’s salt, while on others, they returned to their usual flavor. 

I watched his leathery hand tighten around the blackened bottle of soy sauce, tipping it to allow a mahogany stream into the pot of scrambled eggs. But just as quickly, Grandpa's hand began to tremble- large, sudden jerks that sent a tide of soy sauce tumbling and sloshing over the porcelain egg yolk surface. His brows furrowed in frustration, lips pressed into a thin line as the look of defeat crept into the crevices of his face. 

This was a harsh reminder of his worsening Parkinson’s. Each day, I watched helplessly as he lost more control. 

	Years later, as I stood in front of an operating table, draped in a white lab coat with latex gloves clinging uncomfortably to my skin, I thought back to those moments with Grandpa– the agonizing rigidity in his elbows and the uncontrollable tremors plaguing the pair of hands that had taken care of me for countless years. Armed with a cotton swab in one hand and forceps in another, I worked carefully to locate the bregma– a small, delicate cross on the skull that marked the spot where I would induce traumatic brain injury. Never had I imagined myself in a white coat, especially not on a random Monday afternoon with a Sprague Dawley rat mounted before me. Yet, as I searched for answers in the brain of this tiny creature, I realized that I was no longer a witness to neurodegeneration; I was now contributing to scientific research, driven by the same determination and empathy that had begun in my Grandpa’s kitchen. 

This was the day that I knew I wanted to be a neurosurgeon. 

I fell in love with the artistry of surgery, of tailoring the intricate biology of the brain with scalpels and sutures to potentially restore– or explore– its functions. During a graduate course lecture on the development of feral neural circuits, I encountered the subject of neural tube defects- a congenital condition that causes deformities of the central nervous system. These defects, often linked to nutritional deficiencies and inadequate maternal care, are especially prevalent in developing regions. As I absorbed the lecture, the images of children born with these disorders reminded me of the helplessness I felt watching my Grandpa’s struggle with Parkinson’s. The familiar ache sharpened my determination to make a difference. My goal became clear: to serve as a doctor without borders, bringing medical care to those who need it the most, where small interventions change entire lives. 

	As I reflect on my journey, my mind travels back to that kitchen, where the scent of steamed eggs filled the air and Grandpa’s trembling hands struggled against a body that was no longer under his control. His strength had inspired my own journey. Just as his cooking brought joy to our family, I now seek to bear the same comfort to others through medicine. What started as a love for a simple meal has grown into a lifelong passion for healing– a path of compassion, dedication, and the pursuit of knowledge that knows no bounds.

topic: Share an essay on any topic of your choice. It can be one you've already written, one that responds to a different prompt, or one of your own design
content:
Dumplings, beef stew, pearl meatballs. Every dish my grandma makes is delicious and is always exactly what everyone craves. How? “My cooking isn’t perfect,” she confessed. “I just understand what everyone likes.” 

I didn’t believe her, because all her dishes are perfect for us. Surely, there has to be a secret ingredient: something that makes everything taste so flawless. It must be her attention to detail. So, I applied the same approach to school. The secret ingredient to academic success must be pristine notes and perfectly outlined study guides. I spent hours writing until my hand cramped. But when my teacher commented that my notes didn’t incorporate the main points of his lessons, my perspective shifted. Slowly, I learned to embrace my rushed, messy handwriting as I quickly scribbled down key points in class. It wasn’t about well-organized notes, but about grasping the essence of what really mattered in the lesson.

My teacher’s remark not only improved my studies but also sparked my journey into teaching and mentorship. I wanted to discover the secret ingredient for helping others to learn. I became a tutor through my school’s service club, teaching English to a Ukrainian student. I crafted lesson plans that captured all the essential concepts from when I first learned English. Nothing stuck for me, but my student struggled to keep up. My so-called “secret ingredient” was making her anxious.

Rather than pushing through with my rigid plan, I asked about her favorite movies and used clips from those films to create new lessons. With this personalized approach, she became more engaged and confident as her voice became more steady. All my preparation before had been centered around myself, not her. The real solution was catering to her needs.

Armed with this knowledge, I confidently approached my next mentorship experience as a student assistant in the Dale Carnegie Youth Program. I focused on direct communication, asking my mentees about any struggles they had. But my questions were always met with an awkward silence. I tried calling on students individually, but their responses remained lukewarm.

Perhaps my one-on-one approach with my Ukrainian student didn’t work here. My student knew what she needed, but that’s not the case for everyone. And Grandma has been cooking for decades. Maybe even she didn’t have all the answers right away. And how could I best support these students when even they didn’t know what they needed? Did Grandma adjust and experiment with her recipes until she found what worked?

So I tried experimenting with different methods, until I found that mind maps and visual aids were the dish that satisfied their palate, where they engaged the most in class. That was the breakthrough: they were visual learners. They became more comfortable and engaged in class. Mentorship isn’t about having all the answers – it’s about adjusting to the needs of others, especially when your students don’t yet know what works for them.

I carried this into my internship at NaoNow, an EdTech startup focused on customized English learning. Now, with hundreds of students, a one-size-fits-all curriculum was not feasible. I trained chatbots to create lesson plans tailored to different proficiency levels. We implemented AI systems that provided personalized feedback on speaking skills, offering detailed insights into any student’s learning style. I realized the potential for AI to improve education: I could help make learning more accessible by embracing different styles of learning, focusing on progression, not perfection.

I didn’t understand what my grandma meant back then. Through all of these experiences, however, I’ve come to realize that there is no secret ingredient. Like with my grandma’s cooking, the key is understanding who you’re serving and what they prefer. And it’s the same with teaching and learning. I hope to continue finding new ways to mentor and learn with others, sharing the same joy and warmth that Grandma always brought to our table.

topic: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
content:
In 11th grade, my mother's friend, who is a pastor at the only Baptist Church in Jibei (a small island off the west coast of Taiwan), mentioned they had run out of funds to help underprivileged children get their education. At the time, I had just developed a passion for business and was itching to create my own brand. I founded the Charity Shop, an online business dedicated to selling off miscellaneous items my family gathered over the years. I thought I could donate the proceeds to help the pastor; that way, I could do something I loved while also helping a family friend.

After sending them my first paycheck, I began to wonder what kind of people received my scholarship and if my money could truly help them, so I flew alone to Jibei over summer vacation. 

The bright blue ocean stretched beneath me, sparking my curiosity about life away from the city. Upon arriving by boat, I was struck by the absence of traffic lights, the carefree scooter riders without helmets, and the scarcity of cars. The landscape lacked tall buildings, and the entire island was devoid of supermarkets and department stores. Growing up in Hsinchu, where the tech industry is booming and the buildings are rarely shorter than several floors, I found it refreshing but unsettling to see an uninterrupted horizon line stretch across my field of vision.

On my first night, the lady who ran the family hotel left to collect drinkable water from a dedicated water station. It opened my eyes to how inconvenient life can be in the remote areas of Taiwan. The next day, when I visited the only convenience store on Jibei, I noticed their female hygiene products were severely lacking in selection and overpriced. I realized for the first time the limited scope of my worldview. I grew up in a comfortable bubble and it had just shattered.

That evening, after I met with the children who received my scholarship money, the pastor shared each of their stories with me. Many of the children struggled with gambling fathers, absent parents, and poverty. These circumstances made it more difficult for them to focus on school and create opportunities for themselves. There was only one girl in recent years who escaped the Jibei cycle of poverty. Despite losing her father and having a suicidal mother, she studied hard and earned over $6,000 USD in scholarships for college. Though her relatives gambled it away, she was able to pursue her education away from the island thanks to donations from the church. 

I never knew there were additional challenges brought by financial and familial instability. I learned that some of my peers have to face significant personal hurdles, which actively prevent them from academic success. It is not for lack of effort that many of them fail: rather, forces I had not even known existed are actively working against them. With this new perspective in mind, I came to the conclusion that funding education can help the children on the island transcend the cycle of poverty: my donations are useful to alleviate their financial insecurities so they can focus on studying. 

With this new understanding of the challenges faced by underprivileged students, I am even more determined to recreate the strong sense of community, support, and warmth that the church fostered. I want to become a house that provides fire and hot meals during a snowstorm and welcomes everyone indiscriminately. The spaces I create are to help people find solace and collective strength. Improving myself and the community around me, I want to promote a positive cycle of mutual help and respect, so that more people can become shelters for those in need. College is, I hope, a stepping stone toward this goal of broadening my perspectives and pursuing a profession that enriches myself and society alike.

topic: Share an essay on any topic of your choice. It can be one you've already written, one that responds to a different prompt, or one of your own design
content:
Under the glare of fluorescent hospital lights, a symphony plays—steady beeps of heart monitors are our conductor, the technology’s metronomic backdrop filling the small space. Nurses' feet glide across the linoleum floor, flat shoes whispering like violins. The nearby ventilators sing with asynchronous whooshes, breathing uneasy life into the clinical orchestra. 

Playing at Doernbecher Hospital had become my favorite gig, putting on concerts for pediatric care units. Violin in hand, surrounded by three of my closest friends, I look forward to the dynamic soundscape’s unique stage. 

The crowd erupts into sudden applause, dozens of parents and children breathing in our music, a much-needed respite from the week. In the back, one boy’s hands don’t clap, tubes snaking around his waist; ivy clinging to stone. And yet, he seems fixated on every note, melodies speaking to him in ways words couldn’t.

After the concert, one of my fellow musicians said she had family friends in a room nearby, and we tagged along for support. Inside the room was the boy from the audience, medical devices providing an unnerving white noise permeating the background. We get the story from his parents—Raiden suffers from UBA5, an extraordinarily rare disease with only 38 known cases. As the sickness slowly destroys all basic motor functions, it leaves his parents in a near-impossible race against time, desperately hoping to raise funds to send Raiden to Boston to find a miracle cure for his almost-unprecedented disease.  

As we talked, Raiden watched silently, eyes speaking not only of sleepless nights but also of a deeper and unrelenting perseverance. Remembering how captivated Raiden had been from our previous concert, I knew that somehow, music was my key to helping him—I was determined to use it in whatever way I could. But, what could our ten-person ensemble possibly achieve facing one of the world’s most rare and pernicious diseases? 

Our idea quickly coalesced—we’d put on a concert in order to raise funds for the necessary research.  We started small, trying to figure out the venue. Two weeks of persistent outreach and negotiation paid off in a way none of us had expected: an invitation from Nike, to host our once-humble concert in their massive Portland headquarters. As an extra generosity, they offered to double every dollar we raised. 

Our weekly rehearsals soon became our sanctuary, my house’s basement transformed into the world’s tiniest theater. But through increasingly perfected harmonies, our lofty mission remained somewhat elusive. Could we really raise enough money to make a difference? Would my music affect the audience the same way it had touched Raiden?  

The headquarter’s silver interior complemented our instruments’ polished wood frame. Our cases were stacked neatly on the cold, patterned floor. The basement and hospital waiting room we’d become so accustomed to were dwarfed by Nike’s floor-to-ceiling glass walls, skybridge hanging overhead. 

Before we began, I looked at the crowd of parents and children, executives and businessmen, lawyers and teachers: nearly sixty-thousand dollars worth of support for Raiden. As we were about to begin, I caught a glimpse of Raiden and his parents in the audience and my personal goal narrowed: to talk to them. To use our music as the bridge, to assure him that despite this world of noise, he wasn’t alone in this journey.

We become our music; violins, cellos, and violas filling the near-silent atrium with a tapestry of sound. The audience's feet tap the marble floor, stilettos become cymbals, leather soles our bass. My bow is my voice, the violin’s vibrato speaking of hope; intermittent crescendos create an overture to resilience. As the ending nears, I focus only on Raiden, talking to him in notes turned narratives, phrases that weave into stories. 

The crowd erupts into sudden applause, dozens of parents with their children breathing in our music. But I look ahead, gaze reserved for only a single person, his eyes glisten, transfixed by our small high-school band.

topic: The lessons we take from obstacles we encounter can be fundamental to later success. Recount a time when you faced a challenge, setback, or failure. How did it affect you, and what did you learn from the experience?
content:
This is the story of a setback that began with the abundance of confidence accumulating from the previous winnings. In Robotics, setbacks are inevitable. However, as a team leader, I would never expect to lose in seven consecutive games in one of the most essential robotics competitions, the Taiwan Open Robotics Competition. This competition serves as the preliminary round for high school teams, setting the stage for the final round. In a team captain’s inner heart, this setback feels like an abyss, constantly questioning my captaincy, ability, and the decision made at the moment of every single game.

Seven battles in a row we lost. Although each game lasted only about 2 minutes, the self-imposed pressure and self-blame that accompanied each defeat added up exponentially. As the team leader, I felt as if the weight of the world rested on my shoulders. Ironically, I told myself not to concede failure or express my emotions so easily as to maintain a facade of composure, providing guidance and support to my teammates when all I wanted was a moment to vent my own frustrations. As frustrations and blaming started spreading across team members, I was irritated and angry, believing that we should compose ourselves instead of showing weakness. 

These suppressing emotions make me feel as fragile as a glass structure on the verge of shattering. As I went back home, tears went down my eyes. I was no longer able to digest the mood built up with the accumulated frustration. I began to regret the design choices I made for the robot, and I agonized over my lack of nuanced understanding of the programming. I was consumed by anger, self-blame, and frustration. This state of mind lingered for approximately two weeks, and I frequently carried it as a heavy burden.

Amidst moments of despair, my English tutor’s words come to my mind. She advised me to embrace “let it be.” and always envision the worst-case scenario as a beginning. This perspective reminds me that while I cannot change the past, I have the power to shape my response to it. Situations are not always favorable but each advert experience counts and is invaluable. More importantly, positioning myself for the worst-case scenario encourages an ongoing journey of self-discovery and growth. This reminds me of the badminton court. At the moment of losing the game, I let go of the win-and-loss mindset and think about strategy to survive. The worst scenario is just that we lose everything. That’s it! I have nothing to lose already. Surrender to a situation does not mean giving up. That is when a new path and possibility appear. 

The next competition, the TAS Signature Event, came quickly in December. However, I acquired a stronger and more determined mentality from the previous competition. No longer did I blame myself when we lost the game. Making mistakes did not occupy my mind.     Instead, Accepting those previous failures allows me to improve myself and my strategy as a catalyst. I became brave, starting to develop diverse unconstrained small designs. I became confident because I told myself that there was nothing to lose.  

Setbacks like the one I encountered with the Robotics Competition are formidable trials. But I embrace the notion that life unfolds like an enthralling novel, with its exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution. Everything will ultimately pass. Even in times of roughs, we can keep pushing forward toward the climax. Even during the climax, we will eventually return to the very beginning. While the result is disheartening, “let it be,” along with the practice of considering the worst situation serves as a growing catalyst that induces a period of healing, change, and improvement. Setbacks are temporary and inevitable in life, but my response to them can shape my journey.  I now believe that this is not an ending that defines me but rather a transition toward the next stage. It is a stepping stone toward a future defined by resilience and persistence, allowing me to surmount unprecedented challenges next.

topic: Discuss an accomplishment, event, or realization that sparked a period of personal growth and a new understanding of yourself or others.
content:
As the last whistle of the game blew, the Lynbrook girls’ basketball team secured their spot as third in the Santa Clara Valley Athletics League. As the team captain, I felt proud of ourselves and our journey. Like all other teams, it took us some time to grow and come together as one, but I am glad we did not give up on trying. In the end, with all our hard work, we finished third. 

In the beginning, most drills did not go smoothly because most of us were unfamiliar with each other, and it was a challenge when we wanted to pass the ball. As the oldest team member, I told myself that I should do something to break the ice. Therefore, I talked to the coach and asked if we could let everyone come ten minutes earlier than usual for the next week of practice. Despite suspicions, I encouraged everyone to introduce themselves during drills to build a bond. From discussing our hobbies and backgrounds to tactical discussions regarding everyone’s areas for improvement while warming up together, I saw a drastic improvement in our morale, and I was able to lead the drills more smoothly. To work on our shortcomings, such as long-range passing, I would propose the team practice together as we practice passing while running across the court, allowing us to practice stamina and ensure that no one gets left behind. 

A week after that, the coach told me that the team had nominated me as the team captain. Feeling honored, I accepted the offer because I would love to give everything I have to help this team grow. However, new problems arose as practice continued, as people started to forget about the strategies and plays we had planned for our games because there were too many of them. There was one game when one of the guards forgot to run to the corner, disrupting our pace, and we lost that possession. When we had a break between quarters, I pulled the teammate over and started drawing on the whiteboard with arrows indicating each person’s responsibilities to remind everyone where they should go. Realizing that drawing our play on the whiteboard might not be enough, I organized the team into small groups based on our positions on the court, so each group got three guards and two bigs to run plays and point mistakes. 

Going into the league games season, we faced teams that were even more skilled and challenging to play against. Determined to cheer up my team to continue doing our best after losing three games in a row, I asked my coach and the team’s parents to host a team dinner. At the team dinner, I gave a pep talk about how giving up was too early, and we could still win the rest and achieve the goal we set at the start of the season - the top three in our league. Most of the team was encouraged and seemed more energetic in the practices afterward as we returned to a winning streak and regained our confidence. This taught me how to think positively to encourage my teammates and myself. 

As a captain, I learned how to encourage my teammates, preparing me for the rest of high school and leadership experiences as I became more confident leading members while pursuing a shared goal. I believe this leadership engagement will become instrumental as I join the university’s basketball team, care for my teammates, and always be there when they need me.

topic: Reflect on a time when you questioned or challenged a belief or idea. What prompted your thinking? What was the outcome?
content:
“How are you? I’m fine, thank you, and you?”, a typical greeting format in English textbooks for public school students, standard but monotonous. Under the influence of traditional education, I have always believed that the moment we are born, things have already been decided, like the rigid and old-fashioned textbooks I had in elementary school. In the cultural aspect, the family's background determines the type of lifestyle you live in; biologically, the genetic materials inherited from your parents play an essential role in shaping you, from your physical appearance to health conditions. 

Everyone is born with a unique cover and irrevocable content, but if I had the chance to modify my book, I would like to change many aspects of it, like my height. Like many other girls, I dreamed of being a flight attendant and even imagined myself with a formal uniform, saying, "Welcome aboard." However, when the pediatrician, after looking at my X-ray, announced, “156 at most,” the sudden news hit me like an earthquake, completely unexpected. 

To grasp the 10% possibility of growing taller, my mom offered me a 200-day jumping rope streak challenge to stimulate my growth plate, in exchange for a dog. A cuddle buddy that I have been asking for every birthday. Honestly, I wasn’t expecting growth, but it was a good deal since I could at least get a dog. To my surprise, with day-by-day effort, I can proudly announce myself as the “tallest” female member, alongside a new family member that wiggles its tail and welcomes you with the warmest hug every day. 

From my point of view, my dog is one of the happiest dogs, and its daily routine makes me jealous: eat, sleep, and play. If I have the choice, I hope to reincarnate as a dog, relaxing and carefree. However, this thought only lasted until I watched the Twelve Nights, a documentary that displayed the darkest side of animal shelters in Taiwan. Due to inadequate spaces and food, the dogs confined in animal shelters, if not adopted, will be euthanized after twelve days. Those dogs have glimmering eyes like every other, but a completely different story for their lives. Just like how I changed my own story, I joined the volunteering team at a public animal shelter to rewrite others'. 

During weekends, I walk dogs or accompany them in an open field. Every time they play outside, my team records clips of them playing and posts them on social media, increasing their chances of being seen. Although the overall adoption rate increases, it still struggles to catch up with the growing number of strays. After a conversation with the chief of the shelter, I realized that the root cause can be traced to education. Just as foreigners often view Taiwanese as friendly, our education system emphasizes the idea of willingness to help, yet often overlooks how this value should be applied to multifaceted issues. 

In terms of stray animals, many people feed them out of kindness. However, this unintentionally leads to community issues: uncleaned excrement, continuous barking, and even incidents of attack, all of 

which worsen the public's conception of stray animals. As the government started to advocate for TNR (trap, neuter, release), I joined as an assistant in volunteer training sessions to contribute to spreading this idea. Although some condemned it as a violation of animal rights, it’s currently the most feasible solution in preventing unfortunate lives from entering a society that cannot support them. At the same time, I continued my work in sharing shelter dogs online, hoping that one day, everyone would find their destined home. 

Now, my book has been slowly modified as I tried to change others and has added a new quote on its first page: “Little effort matters.” With more challenges ahead, I’m ready to turn to the next chapter and continue sharing my story to reach and influence more readers.

topic: Share an essay on any topic of your choice. It can be one you've already written, one that responds to a different prompt, or one of your own design
content:
